label: Analysis
tagline: Understand the problem before you design the solution.

whatItIs.beginner: Analysis is the first phase of ADDIE. It's where you stop and ask: "What is the real problem here, and is training actually the answer?" Before you design a single slide or activity, you need to understand your learners, their environment, and what success looks like. Think of it as doing your homework before the project starts.
whatItIs.advanced: The Analysis phase is a needs assessment and performance gap analysis combined. You are diagnosing root causes — determining whether the performance gap is due to a lack of knowledge/skill (a training problem), a motivation issue, an environmental barrier, or a process failure. Only knowledge/skill gaps are solved by training. Everything else requires a non-training intervention. A rigorous analysis also surfaces task hierarchies, prerequisite knowledge, and transfer conditions that will later drive your instructional strategy.

whyItMatters.beginner: Skipping Analysis is the number-one reason training fails. If you build a course before understanding your audience and the real problem, you might create something no one needs — or something too hard, too easy, or aimed at the wrong people. Analysis saves time and budget by making sure you're solving the right problem.
whyItMatters.advanced: Skipping or rushing Analysis is the leading cause of ineffective learning solutions. Without it, you risk designing solutions for symptoms rather than root causes, over-scoping content, and producing learning that doesn't transfer to the job. Analysis data directly feeds your instructional strategy, your evaluation plan (Level 3 and Level 4 of Kirkpatrick), and your performance objectives. Getting it right here multiplies the quality of every downstream phase.

keyQuestions: What is the performance gap? What are people doing vs. what should they be doing?
Is this a training problem, or is it something else (process, tools, motivation, environment)?
Who are the learners? What do they already know? What are their attitudes toward the topic?
What constraints exist — time, budget, technology, delivery format?
What does success look like? How will we know training worked?
What is the organizational context and urgency of the problem?
What subject matter experts (SMEs) are available and how much of their time can we access?
outputs: Needs Assessment Report or summary memo
Project Scope Agreement or Statement of Work

commonMistakes.fix: Always diagnose before you prescribe. Hold a discovery meeting before agreeing to any deliverable.
Ask whether learners need to build a new skill or just access information on the job. Job aids beat courses for reference tasks.

commonMistakes.mistake: Only talking to managers, not learners
commonMistakes.fix: Go to the source. Learner interviews and observations reveal realities that managers don't always see.
Surfacing budget, timeline, and tech constraints in Analysis prevents painful scope changes in Develop.
Quantify the gap where possible. "Sales reps close 12% fewer deals than peers" is more useful than "sales reps need training."

advancedInsights.heading: When to compress or skip formal needs analysis
advancedInsights.body: Urgent compliance deadlines, regulatory mandates, or situations where the problem is already thoroughly documented by the business can justify a compressed analysis. In those cases, replace a full needs assessment with a one-hour discovery meeting and a written scope agreement that documents what you know and what you're assuming. You're not skipping analysis — you're recording it faster. If you proceed without any analysis, you own the misalignment risk.

advancedInsights.heading: Running async analysis with distributed SMEs
advancedInsights.body: For geographically distributed teams, tools like Miro, MURAL, or Microsoft Whiteboard let SMEs and learner reps contribute to a needs analysis asynchronously. Use pre-structured templates: a 'current state / desired state / root cause' board that participants populate over 48 hours beats a single 90-minute meeting where the loudest voice dominates.

advancedInsights.heading: Government and DoD contexts
advancedInsights.body: In government and DoD environments, Analysis often includes a formal Training Needs Analysis (TNA) or Front-End Analysis (FEA) report that follows a specified format — such as TRADOC Regulation 350-70 — and requires command approval before Design can begin. Skipping or abbreviating this step is a contract compliance issue, not just an ID judgment call.

advancedInsights.heading: See also: SAM and Rapid Prototyping
advancedInsights.body: SAM compresses Analysis into a brief Preparation phase, operating on the assumption that requirements will emerge through prototyping. This is a legitimate trade-off when the cost of extended analysis outweighs the cost of early iteration — but it requires experienced IDs who can make fast design decisions without a full data set.

caseStudy.situation: Meridian Manufacturing's VP of Sales flagged a problem: the sales team was losing roughly 30% of enterprise deals at the "technical deep dive" stage — the point in a sales call when buyers asked detailed questions about product specifications and configurations. The L&D team was asked to build a training solution.
caseStudy.approach: The instructional designer conducted 8 SME interviews — 5 with top-performing sales reps and 3 with product engineers — and a survey of 40 reps. She also reviewed 6 months of lost-deal notes from the CRM. The pattern that emerged: reps knew the product line existed but couldn't confidently explain specifications, configurations, or competitive differentiators. The root cause was a knowledge gap, not motivation — reps were eager to sell but had never received formal product training. Analysis also revealed that just 3 of the company's 14 product families accounted for 80% of the lost deals.
caseStudy.outcome: The team recommended a blended solution — e-learning for core product knowledge, a companion quick-reference job aid, and an optional VILT Q&A session — rather than a job aid alone (reps needed internalized knowledge, not just reference material they'd never open mid-call). Scoping the project to 3 product families instead of all 14 saved an estimated 8 weeks of development time and gave the team a defensible rationale when the stakeholder pushed for broader coverage.
